facts_disclosed: Looking at second location in Germantown

turns.content: hey quick question. you know anything about opening a second location for a small business
I can definitely help think through that. What kind of business are you running, and what's making you consider a second location?
auto shop. tires and general repair. been running the one spot for a while and business has been picking up enough that i think theres room to grow

That's a good sign when demand is pushing you to think bigger. A few key things to consider: Can your current location run without you being there every day? Do you have someone you trust to manage one of the two spots? And do you have a specific area in mind, or are you still scouting?
been looking at a spot in Germantown for a second shop. the rent's not bad and theres good traffic over there. lot of families with cars that need work

Germantown is a solid area for that. Higher household income, lots of families with multiple vehicles, and people there tend to value convenience and trust with their mechanic over chasing the cheapest price. That works in your favor if you do quality work. What's the space look like — is it already set up as a shop or would you need to build it out?
its an old quick lube place that closed down. already has 3 bays and a lift. would need some work but nothing crazy. maybe 15-20k to get it right

That's a huge advantage. Converting a space from scratch can run you 50K or more easily, so starting with bays and a lift already in place saves you a lot. Just make sure you get the lift inspected before you commit — used lifts can be a liability if they haven't been maintained. What's the lease situation looking like? Monthly rent and term length?
they want 3200 a month. 3 year lease with option to renew. i think thats fair for the area but man its a big commitment when you already got overhead at the first shop

3200 for Germantown with three bays sounds reasonable. But you're right to be cautious about carrying two rents. The smart move is to figure out your break-even number for the new location — how many cars a day you'd need to cover rent, utilities, payroll for at least one tech, and supplies. If that number feels realistic for the area, you're in good shape. Have you run those numbers yet?
roughly yeah. i figure i need about 4 cars a day to break even. 6 or 7 to actually make money. germantown has the traffic for it i think but the first few months are gonna be tight

Four cars a day to break even is doable, especially in a suburb like that where people need reliable service close to home. The first three to six months are always the hardest for a second location. One thing that helps is building up a few months of operating expenses as a cushion before you open the doors, so you're not panicking if week two is slow. Can you set aside a reserve, or would you be running tight from day one?
thats the part im working on. trying to figure out the financing side of things. but the opportunity feels right. i dont want to sit on it and watch somebody else take that space

That urgency makes sense. Good commercial spaces in good locations don't sit empty long. Just make sure the financing is locked down before you sign anything. If the numbers work and you can cover three months of overhead as a safety net, it sounds like a move worth making. Better to grow when demand is pulling you forward than to wait and play catch-up later.
yeah thats how i see it too. appreciate the help man. gives me some stuff to think through this week
Anytime. You're asking the right questions, which means you're approaching it the right way. Good luck with the space — sounds like it could be a real opportunity.
